item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think skips that've been neutralized no longer require containment of any sort, either because they've become 100% non-anomalous or been completely destroyed. While none of the anomalous functions of the exclusion harness remain functional, the Foundation can't just melt it down or throw it away because it's made of classified Foundation tech and contains sensitive information about some kind of crazy VK-class event or something. It still needs to be kept on hand for future reference and out of the hands of enemies, which is still containment of a sort, so it's considered Safe.

That's my two cents, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hold on. Why is this safe and not neutralized? like seriously it's anomalous effects are gone.
This is great, great atmosphere, etc… All of this has been said before.

item.title: Re: Why destroy SCP-2000?
item.content:encoded: Why did I bother to ask… Nevermind.

item.title: Re: Why destroy SCP-2000?
item.content:encoded: Strechy boi? What? The Foundation is trying to exterminate the human species or maybe even all life on earth because they discovered the same fundamental fact about life that makes 682 despise us so much. They blew up 2000 because they want to prevent anyone from repopulating the earth.

item.title: Why destroy SCP-2000?
item.content:encoded:
This is something i just dont get; i understand why the foundation wants to kill everyone (because of IT, AKA strechy boi), but why destroy SCP-2000? it makes no sense. In order to explain it, i want you to imagine this: Pietro Wilson fails to deliver 055 to 579, the foundation manages to kill everone in the planet thus successfully killing strechy boi and "saving" humanity, but what's next for the foundation? Oh! lets repopulate earth with SCP-2000- oh right… we destroyed it by triggering the yellowstone eruption, not to mention that said eruption might cause a chain reaction that would trigger the rest of the supervolcanoes worldwide… oops.

Also where is the chaos insurgency?

Don't get me wrong, this SCP is great but… Destroying SCP-2000 is confusing… At least for me.

item.title: Happy 5000 day
item.content:encoded: Today's the day, also I have only one thing to say after finishing. Jesus fucking Christ.

item.title: SCP-5000 will be discovered tomorrow on 12/4/20
item.content:encoded:
Anyways nice article, one of my favorites in Series 6.

5/5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So much Tale potential here. Did Gears survive? Where's Clef? Who's been performing Procedure 110-Montauk during all this? What about the indestructible humanoids like Cain? What's a kaijumancer? I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's very interesting how SCP-5000 organizes the site's history and sends a respect for the classics.

And the hidden twist was shocking enough to be angry

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the presence of hidden text. I had to re-read it again to take a fresh look at what I had written.

item.title: lookcloser.png
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm…

+ show block
– hide block

please collapse your images

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this, really showed all the power the foundation has on it's hands, nice homage.

Also found pretty funny the part about how removing the mask to talk defeats the purpose, given nowadays context.

My headcanon shall be that this anomaly is trying to show the Foundation that it needs to do everything in it's power to never be compromised on this level. Since the agent came back alive and with all the info, this felt like a warning.

item.title: Awesome SCP
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is just amazing in every way, and it inspires me to be more creative with writing.

+1

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: "We've got a live one" is a decently common phrase on its own. Iirc its a reference to fishing, when a Fisherman get a fish on the hook, they will often say "We've got a live one" often meaning that they are gonna have to fight to pull it in. Generally it means you've found something you were looking for. Or to go along with the fishing root, think as it as meaning that you "found a good catch."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think most of the obvious routes are being prevented by some of the other GoIs.

I've seen some people talking about "Why didn't they just break down SCP-2935 and get people to run in and out of it?" to which I responded "Probably because 'Nobody' would've met them at the door with a gun."

Not that 'Nobody' Specifically would've been there, but the point is the world wasn't exactly going down without a fight.

Even assuming that the GOC, Serpents Hand, and CoTBG were all defeated for various reasons, there are still other people who would've been preventing the end of the world:

'Nobody', MC&D, The Fifth Church (Maybe), the MCF, some sarkic tribes, Pangolin, the UIU (And other localized anomaly-hunting organizations), Anderson Robotics, the Chicago Spirit, The Chaos Insurgency (Really surprised they didn't show up here), the 3 Moons Initiative (Depending on your head-cannon), Wilson's Wildlife Solutions, Some of the craftier Para-watch people (Maybe), renegade Foundation employees, Wilson's Wildlife Solutions, Prometheus Labs, etc, etc, etc. I could go on for days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP provides such a interesting take of an XK end of the world type scenario; where the foundation itself were cause of it. or perhaps they weren't? Regardless of how this SCP ends; there is so much mystery i love about SCP's that i got instantly hooked.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I've been thinking a lot about this one after reading it a few days ago to figure out what was bothering me about it, and I think I've gotten it.

+ show block
– hide block

The threat that the Foundation identifies is associated with the psychosphere, which implies that it's limited to humans, or at the very least, to sapience. That's where this thing seems to exist, or is otherwise relevant. The Foundation decided it was such a threat that the only solution was to wipe out its food source (or whatever) by killing off people. I get that much.

But the problem is that the SCPs they released were WAY OVERQUALIFIED for simply wiping out humanity. 682, for instance, is specifically described as having a hatred for "all life." 610 is also implied to affect non-human life. I assume that the SCPs listed in this article are just a fraction of what was actually happening out there, and that the implication is that every SCP, or at least most of them, were let loose. Why not? If people are gone, nobody's going to be around to contain them anyway.

So that means the reality-destroyers. That means apocalyptic events going uncontained. And so forth.

So this is where the ethics question comes in. An ethical decision has to benefit SOMETHING. So how did the ethics committee decide that it was worth releasing SCPs that would leave literally nothing alive on the planet? What is benefiting from that decision? I would understand if one or two SCPs that specifically targeted human beings were released, because at least then, there would be a planet full of life left, and there could be an ethical argument to be made that saving life itself is worth the sacrifice of humanity.

But if the only reward for a decision is a lifeless ball of rock with nothing left alive on it because you released every indiscriminately-dangerous SCP, then that's not even a Pyrrhic victory. That's simply a failure. If there is nothing left to benefit from an ethical decision, then it's hard to accept that this was, in fact, an ethical decision, and that the Ethics Committee would have had any reason to sign off on this.

The only way this decision could have made sense is if The Evil Thing was able to affect life outside of the psychosphere, which doesn't seem implied by the article. Because otherwise, you're wiping out every unaffected species for absolutely no reason, since there are more than a few SCPs that could single-handedly end human life on this planet without affecting anything else, and releasing just one or two of those would have been a far more ethical decision than releasing everything.

It's very well written and an enjoyable read, but the entire horror of the article seems heavily weighted on the experts deciding that killing literally everything on this planet (and perhaps beyond) was the most ethical decision. If I can't accept that decision based on the scenario that's presented in the article, then it starts to feel a bit like a "what if" showcase of the SCP Rogue's Gallery rather than having a genuine, immersive reason to have happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite SCPs, it’s an interesting idea and the story is well told. I wasn’t familiar with all the referenced SCPs but I read their pages as I made my way through. It feels like taking a walk through the foundation, a very foreboding walk.

5000, you bastard, how on earth did you get here in merely 8 months?

To all past upvotes and all future ones!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me or does make it seem like SCP-049 may have been correct in saying that there is some kind of disease in us. Like I don't think that's the way this SCP was going but if you think about it, when SCP-049 'removes' the disease they either highly aggressive or don't do anything, however they don't seem to show pain or emotion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But they don't know that SCP-3125 can kill people. They don't even know anything about what 3125 does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure that if the foundation actually decided to kill everyone for whatever reason, then there are probably like eight dozen different SCPs that could do it in under an hour. Just chisel away at the marble pillars holding 2317-K in place, for example. Or tell everyone about 3125.

item.title: small discrepancy with dates
item.content:encoded: Decided to read through 4290's file since I didn't know what it was. Apparently this thing's corpse only surfaced in 2022, but the files take place around 2020. Not important, just figured it was worth mentioning

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have there been any tales made about this world yet? I'd be interested in reading a few.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow,5k!
Haha,my 579 is black
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this scip so unique and love the alternate reality that unfolds here. It shows how scary how vulnerable it makes us when our safety is in the hands of a private company. +1

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Loved the ending. He understands why humanity must be eradicated, but it is still unknown to the audience like the "pestilence" for 049. If we knew why the O5 made this decision the ending wouldn't hit as hard. And it all links back to an SCP that on first reading seems boring or annoying to read but paired with this article makes for great storytelling. Great work Tanhony.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know it's bad when 2000 gets destroyed lol.

Anyway, loved this thing, actually got me back into SCP what with all the references to other stuff! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an absolute joy to read, it really is. I can't avoid being bummed out in the end, because the feeling of wanting to know more about this story is strong, and I didn't like that at first. But thinking about it I realize how much effort you put into architecting this piece to be exactly like this, and I kinda of love it exactly as it is right now. +1

item.title: Neat.
item.content:encoded: Hey I haven't read an SCP in a few years, but I got directed this way by a reference on reddit, nice to see that there is still good work being done on here.

item.title: I love everything about this.
item.content:encoded:
Exclusion Site: You leave the outside world to avoid reality altering events
The Suit: You can enter the outside world and still avoid reality altering events.

SCP-055: The reader is given all available information, but there isn't any because the object makes you forget.
SCP-579: There is a lot of information about the object, but it's all redacted so the reader knows nothing.

You took two sets of opposite concepts and put them together. Beautiful.

item.title: Re: What about the immortal people?
item.content:encoded: I guarantee 781 was terminated. Likely multiple times.

item.title: What about the immortal people?
item.content:encoded: So it is said that all human and human-sympathetic anomalies were terminated. So how about SCP-073 "Cain", who cannot be harmed; SCP-076 "Abel" and SCP-781, who will just respawn?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it doesn't go back in time with you. Otherwise, they'd just keep piling up every time somebody used it and we'd have a whole warehouse full of non-spheres.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I just figured the knife test soldiers were zombies or some other form of dead thing made by some skip. They don't say "We've got a guy who can feel pain." They say "We've got a live one."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, so the people whose job it is to pump the breaks are instead slamming on the gas, causing widespread chaos and mass deaths, the guy who sacrificed everything changed nothing, and we, the audience, don't even get a reason why? I don't get it. How are we supposed to take anything away from a story like this? What's the bloody point?

(turns on the news)

Ohhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got to the end of the article and realized…
The description says the suit and the body appeared in 579's containment chamber.
But no briefcase.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the tale is nice but the item, well…
why is this an scp?(no offense)
the suit is not anomalous isn't it?
the files aren't infohazardous and the suit is broken, and the only odd thing is "body of a living guy" which can be explained by it being from another universe, and there is no point of special containment procedures. just a broken suit and the only reason to "contain"(store) it is to stop the public from knowing the foundation. as is a cup with foundation logos on it.
cleverly avoids clinical tone by LOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOONG addendum
bored "Ivan-Readerski-Kommentov"s find out the truth before the foundation which is supposed to have stronger computers,more resources etc.
good story but sarificed the item itself and has two major bugs, therefore it wins a down from me. sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how is it not an scp article if its formatted exactly like one

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Well, their objective was to keep the anomalous entities from the public eye to keep the normalcy of the world. But now they want to destroy humanity, so there's no reason to hide them anymore because there's no one to hide them from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But it is simply not an SCP article.

Well, if you look at the mainlist, it actually is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't impressed.

This is a good tale but a poor SCP.

+ show block
– hide block

First, it is a misdirect. The skip isn't the suit, it isn't even related to the suit. The skip is whatever eldritch horror the foundation is trying to stop or has been corrupted by. Quite frankly, the suit is the mcguffin that allows the tale to be told. Reading the article, Wilson could've died any number of ways except his magic lurker device always makes him immune.

Second, the article reads like a playlist of SCP's greatest hits. All the interesting parts of the article are derived from allowing the reader's imagination to play with the many horrors running loose. Neither the horror nor suit have any bearing on the story other than to give an ambiguous reason for it to exist and be observed.

Along with the action being derived via crosslink, so is the dialogue. Naturally, in an entire world beset by apocalypses, our protagonist would happen across the one amulet wearing crowd favorite who can deliver the right mix of nostalgia and exposition.

Fourth, the article takes advantage of the old "magic thing in a box" style to make the new narrative driven style more appealing without any effort. 055 is blatantly used to omit large portions of time, taking for granted the reader's willingness to head canon insert amazing adventures into the gaps. Simultaneously, 055 is the perfect mcguffin to save the world with. What will it do? What is it? I dunnu, but it's gunna save the world! Except, (What A Twist!) it's actually what the evil thing wants ?(question mark)? In any case, as the works on the site deviate more and more from peanut's inspirational format they still rely on old standbys to hook the reader. This while young people inspired to write their own magic item skips are waved off dismissively.

Fifth, Wilson is seriously going to take the time to put together tables and lists of anomalies and how they're being deployed? I could see a person making a log, a journal entry, about these, but the F*G world is ending and he's making tables. The guy is a technician even, not one of the hardass researchers who might have the habit to do that. It's minor, but it just shines more light on how crosslink driven the tale is.

Every x000 till this one added or cemented a defining characteristic to the SCP universe. This adds nothing, at best. At worst, it demolishes the SCP universe. This is because, at best, what you get out of the article is the existence of a lurker suit that lets you investigate SCPs without them killing/maiming/mentally damaging you. At worst, the new precedent for the SCP universe is that humankind/normalcy is actually the anomaly and the purpose of the SCP organization is/should be to destroy it.

How do I qualify this? It's a decent piece of literature. It is a great piece of SCP fanfiction for the in-crowd. But it is simply not an SCP article.

item.title: Absolutely impressive.
item.content:encoded: Well-deserving of the 5k slot. This embodies everything that makes a masterful SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The name of this article remind me of Tommy Wiseau, upvoted.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Hey, good game, man. The SCP-5000 contest was a fun one, and I felt good playing against, and losing to, you. Great SCP, and honestly terrifying. May this never happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the thing 682 (and the Foundation) wants to exterminate SCP-3002?

item.title: Re: But the actual monster was shown
item.content:encoded:
in any real way.

This still does not count for me. Again, me personally. But no, I do not see this as satisfying.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This may be the best SCP on the site if ever read. I reread the entire thing until I finally feel that I have a moderate understanding. So here we go! (Also quick warning for spelling and grammar errors)
+ show block
– hide block

There is an entity in the minds of every human that is not naturally occurring and is responsible for pain, suffering, ect. SCP-682 possess knowledge about this entity and this explains it's desire to fuck over the human race.
The Overseer Council and ethics agree that is would be a mercy to kill all humans compared to what this entity has in mind.
SCP-682 either finds a way to take a human body or comes into contact with a mimetic agent that makes it seem like he's a human. He was then interrogated and forms back into his normal shape and eats them. What made me think he ate them was the line at the end, "Distrusting". All the references to 682 work very well as to not make the reader shrug off the article for including the classic. I know I'm getting a little off topic but the part about where he's listing anomalies that have been turned on the humans and you see some famous scips like 097 and 662, and you know what's coming next, "SCP-682 | Released" That one word sent chills up my spine. Anyway I'll get back onto topic.
055 + 579 = Restart time to before the crisis happened.
Our hero manages to reset the timeline but heroically sacrifices himself to the good of humanity… But wait, if the Overseers and Ethics agreed that this was a mercy, does that now mean humans are headed straight for whatever is worse than Plagues, Blunt force trauma, a giant fucking lizard, and memetic kill agents, then the world is on a highway to hell.
I loved this piece and I think it also helped the lore for 579, 055 and, 682. They didn't really need help but I feel like now 682 isn't just a lizard that fucking hates humans, it may be he's trying to save them, and is pissed because we don't realize he's saving us, I also made a connection to 049, is this the true pestilence? I think this is underrated even at 4 digits. Also all the other series-1 anomalies didn't make me cringe, it was actually exiting to see them in there, I'm getting repetitive so here's my two cents, Best. Article. Ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just want to say that, out of all the SCP articles I've read, this is probably the one that terrifies me the most. I'm not saying that just because of the idea of the Foundation turning on humanity (that announcement gave me chills), but also…

+Spoilers
-Spoilers

So we can safely say that something exists in humanity that isn't supposed to be there and that it was dangerous enough for O5 and the ethics committee to agree that humanity needed to be destroyed to deal with it. The question is: does this still exist in the universe/timeline that "our" Foundation recovered the suit with the apocalypse log?
And the fact that we don't know makes it all the more terrifying.

You probably get a lot of comments about this entry, but I still wanted to say thank you for writing it and you did an incredible job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sure this isn't anything that hasn't already been said, but I just wanted to register how much I love that such a simple title, "Why?" can mean so much. It is the implicit question that exists from the very beginning, that the reader asks with every development, and it both summarizes and justifies the story as a whole, and in the end the answer itself is the anomaly. Definitely my favorite thing about the article, which is undoubtedly one of the best-written ones on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're missing the forest for the trees.

+ show block
– hide block

Something fundamental within humanity is so terrible that eradicating all of it is a preferable alternative, remember? It's what happens if they DON'T.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good god, that story was something else. I feel sorta queasy for being "soft" and empathetic, now.

+ My Thoughts
- Hide Spoilers

What's been discovered within humanity's collective unconscious that are not only linked to two of the most distinctly human traits, but so horrific that eradicating humanity would be a mercy? Just what is it that 682 sees that is cured? What are the full ramifications of being cured? Did Sam Ross reveal "IT" to Morrison and Rhodes? Why oh why are empathy and pain linked to this entity? Is the distorted being what lies within the collective unconscious?

"That's why you hear your voice. We're so kind to you, you know. We fight in the light so you can die in the dark."

I haven't felt this internally disturbed since SCP-610. It feels like there's something more to it that has yet to be explained/uncovered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never geeked out so hard over an SCP article before, the concept alone is badass. The way some SCPs are combined to be even deadlier and how the Foundation is cold and unrelenting in its assault against humanity is just beautiful.

item.title: Re: Why?
item.content:encoded: you good?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon: The Foundation does what it does because they witnessed the full scope (or at least an aspect significant to humanity) of SCP-1193/SCP-1877.

item.title: But the actual monster was shown
item.content:encoded:
It was on the horizon, like a person stretched out - no, that's not the best way to describe it. It was like the space around them was stretched out, and they were being stretched along with it, like some kind of bad photo-shop effect. Their body went from the ground up to the clouds, and their jaw swung at right angles. There were these gaps, as well, black gaps in space around its body, like wings. It just floated forwards like that.
There were Foundation guys there, too, but they were fighting it, shooting it with guns and rockets. How fucked up is it that I'm thinking the Foundation fighting an anomaly is weird? Maybe they were like me, managed to get out when this all kicked off. Thought about talking to them, but decided not to. Can't risk it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seeing a lot of people say stuff like "Why didn't they just drop a bunch of nukes and call it a day?"

My response to that would be: Because sentences like

SCP-3199 eggs are now being airdropped pretty much wherever.

and

SCP-682 | Released.

are cooler to read.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I was really shocked. up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, right, the logs, just remembered those.
